title: Income share of the richest 10% (after tax)
subtitle: The share of income received by the richest 10% of the population. Income here is measured after taxes and benefits.
caption:
Income share of the richest 10% (after tax)

The share of income received by the richest 10% of the population. Income here is measured after taxes and benefits.

This line chart tracks the income share of the richest 10% of the population from 1963 to 2024, with income measured after taxes and benefits and equivalized. The vertical axis ranges from 0% to 60%. South Africa, represented by a maroon line, appears only from roughly 2005 to 2018, starting above 50%, peaking near 55% around 2010, then dropping to just under 50% by 2018. Chile’s green line begins near 40% in 1990, rises slightly above 40% around 2000, then declines to approximately 35% by 2024. Brazil’s brown line starts above 40% in the early 1980s, peaks near 47% around 1990, then falls steadily to about 35% by 2024. China’s purple line begins near 30% in 2002, dips slightly below 30% around 2010, then rises back to nearly 30% by 2024. The United States’ blue line remains between 25% and 30% for most of the period, ending near 30% in 2024. France’s orange line stays mostly between 23% and 27%, beginning near 27% in 1970 and ending near 25% in 2024. The lines for China, the United States, and France cluster in the lower half of the chart, generally between 23% and 30%, while Brazil, Chile, and South Africa occupy higher positions, mostly between 35% and 55%. No country exceeds 60% or falls below 20% during the observed periods.
